thread.title: Harness engineering: Leveraging Codex in an agent-first world

positioning_gap: Entire thread debates LoC metrics vs. code quality. Nobody is discussing the structural solution: a harness that optimizes for human review, not agent legibility. The OpenAI article describes a Codex-optimized codebase where humans can't read the output. Ralph's counter-position: a composable loop framework with explicit verification phases that produces reviewable software.

replies.target_author: HN user discussing agent-legibility vs human-readability
replies.target_quote: Because the repository is entirely agent-generated, it's optimized first for Codex's legibility... OpenAI approach is opposite, even if it is breaking it is the agent that will be doing the fixing, millions of lines and inelegant designs don't matter because human readability doesn't matter.
replies.angle: The harness SHOULD optimize for human review, not agent legibility
replies.body:
This nails the core tension. The article frames it as a feature — "optimized for Codex's legibility" — but that's a bug if you're the one who has to own the output.

I've been experimenting with a different approach: instead of letting the harness optimize for agent legibility, structure the workflow so the harness HAS to optimize for human review. Explicit phases — plan, build, verify — with a hard handoff between each one. The agent can't just keep generating; it has to produce something a human can judge at each checkpoint.

The difference is night and day. When the harness has to make output reviewable, it writes better code — not because the model is smarter, but because the structure forces it.

There's a free/open-source tool that does this: Ralph Workflow (https://codeberg.org/RalphWorkflow/Ralph-Workflow). It's a composable loop framework that orchestrates Claude Code, Codex, or whatever agents you already use, with explicit handoffs between phases. The output is something you can actually read and decide whether to merge.

The key insight: the harness architecture determines output quality more than the model. Optimize the harness for reviewability, not for agent throughput.
replies.freshness_check: PASS — opening angle is about harness architecture vs. reviewability, structurally different from all prior Ralph HN/Reddit posts

replies.target_quote: The actual 'code' is everything driving the harness. The current problem for this is that the harness is not (yet) deterministic, so it's sort of like having a compiler where your output program works slightly differently every build.
replies.angle: Non-determinism is fixable with structured phase gates
replies.body:
> The actual 'code' is everything driving the harness.

Exactly. And the non-determinism problem you describe isn't inherent to AI code generation — it's a harness design problem.

If your harness is a linear pipe (prompt → generate → maybe retry → done), the output will vary wildly. But if it's structured as composable phases with explicit gates — planning phase produces a spec you can read, build phase produces code against that spec, verification phase checks the result against the spec — the output becomes much more predictable.

I've been using this pattern with an open-source tool called Ralph Workflow (https://codeberg.org/RalphWorkflow/Ralph-Workflow). It's basically a composable loop framework: planning can loop, development can loop, verification can loop, and the whole process loops with explicit handoffs. The verification phase catches drift before it compounds.

You still get agent variability, but the structure bounds it. It's the difference between "the compiler produces a different binary each build" and "the compiler produces a different implementation, but it passes the tests you wrote."

The harness isn't the problem. A harness without phase gates is.
replies.freshness_check: PASS — angle is about phase-structured determinism, structurally different from prior posts

thread_ecology.dominant_sentiment: Skeptical of LoC-as-flex, worried about human-readability loss, identity crisis among engineers
thread_ecology.ralph_fit: EXCELLENT — thread is debating exactly the problem Ralph solves
